channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Nashville Reporter Released From ICE Custody
item.description: Estefany Maria Rodriguez Florez, who works for a Spanish-language outlet, was released on bond in a case that has raised concerns about press freedom.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: via Araceli Crescencio
item.media:description: Estefany Maria Rodriguez Florez, a Nashville reporter detained by immigration agents.

item.title: Iran Combines Real-World Missile Attacks With Online Threats
item.description: Iran launched a missile strike, a disinformation push and a cyberattack targeting Israel all at the same time, analysts say.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times
item.media:description: Inspecting the aftermath of an Iranian strike in southern Israel on Thursday. Iran also pushed disinformation messaging and launched a cyberattack against Israel on Thursday.

item.title: Trump’s Handpicked Arts Commission Approves Gold Coin With His Face on It
item.description: Many of America’s founders were fiercely against taking steps that would make its government officials appear like kings, and that included featuring them on the country’s coins.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Charlie Savage

item.title: Cesar Chavez Avenue May Soon Be Gone. Yet to Be Confronted: His Legacy.
item.description: After the revelations of sex abuse, the public is left to make sense of the labor leader’s work and life.
item.dc:creator: Richard Fausset
item.media:credit: Jim Thompson/Albuquerque Journal/ZUMA Wire, via Reuters
item.media:description: The Cesar Chavez Day March in 2019 in Albuquerque, N.M.

item.title: Student Loan Office to Leave the Education Department
item.description: The office will move to the Treasury Department as the Trump administration slowly dismantles the agency overseeing federal education policy.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration announced plans to move the nation’s nearly $1.7 trillion student loan portfolio from the Education Department to the Treasury Department.

item.title: Trump’s Complaint About Israeli Strike on Gas Field Exposes Divergent Strategies
item.description: President Trump said he told Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel he disapproved of the attack, which sent energy markets reeling. But Israeli officials said the Americans were informed beforehand.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has been trying to preserve Iran’s oil and gas infrastructure and keep the country from retaliating at energy facilities throughout the Persian Gulf.

item.title: Warren Endorses Platner in Maine, Splitting With Schumer
item.description: The intervention by Ms. Warren is the latest instance of her embracing a candidate at odds with Mr. Schumer, the top Senate Democrat.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Photographs by Allison Robbert for The New York Times and Sophie Park/Getty Images
item.media:description: Senator Elizabeth Warren, Democrat of Massachusetts, has endorsed Graham Platner in an acrimonious moment in the Senate race that is among the most closely watched primaries in 2026.

item.title: Trump’s Planned White House Screening Center Is Too Big, Arts Panel Says
item.description: The Commission of Fine Arts delayed a vote on the facility, a new, 33,000-square-foot security screening center for White House visitors, because of concerns over the design.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Cheriss May for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump does not seem to have the same personal affinity for his proposed visitor center as he does for the ballroom, which he considers to be a legacy project.

item.title: Trump Jokes About Pearl Harbor in Meeting With Japan’s Prime Minister
item.description: Breaking a taboo, President Trump needled Japan’s prime minister about the World War II attack, as she widened her eyes and appeared to take a deep breath in the Oval Office.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Encourages Flow of Iranian Oil While It Battles Iran
item.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said removing sanctions on Iranian oil would lower global prices.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the sanctions exemption would apply to about 140 million barrels of Iranian oil that were currently at sea.

item.title: Patel Says F.B.I. Agents Who Worked Mar-a-Lago Case Were Fired for Ethics Violations
item.description: The assertion by the F.B.I. director to a House committee is likely to be challenged in court.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.category: Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
item.media:credit: Josh Ritchie for The New York Times
item.media:description: The F.B.I. searched President Trump’s Mar-a-Lago estate in 2022, as part of an investigation into his handling of classified documents.

item.title: Lawsuit Accuses Justice Dept. Leadership of ‘Political Retribution’
item.description: The suit filed by two fired F.B.I. agents aimed to hold the Trump administration accountable for the purge of personnel who had worked on the investigations of Mr. Trump or his allies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The lawsuit accused Kash Patel, the F.B.I. director, and Attorney General Pam Bondi of firing the agents because they were deemed “politically disloyal to President Trump.”

item.title: General Says U.S. Boat Strikes Are Not Answer to Country’s Drug Problem
item.description: Gen. Francis L. Donovan, the head of the military’s Southern Command, said he was working with regional allies on the issue.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Getty Images
item.media:description: “Boat strikes will be one of the main tools, and probably not the most effective,” Gen. Francis L. Donovan told Congress on Thursday.

item.title: Mortgage Rates Highest in Three Months as War Weighs on Housing Market
item.description: Rising energy costs and concerns about inflation have helped lift borrowing costs for a home mortgage for the third consecutive week.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Aaron Agosto for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mortgage rates dipped below 6 percent in February but have since climbed as rising energy costs and inflation fears have pushed up the yield on Treasury bonds.

item.title: Pentagon Seeks Additional $200 Billion to Fund Iran War
item.description: The request, which the White House has not yet submitted to Congress, is already encountering some resistance.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Tony Romm, Karoun Demirjian and Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Mandel Ngan/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth speaking to reporters on Thursday.

item.title: Iran War Narrows Fed’s Path to Rate Cuts as Trump Demands Immediate Relief
item.description: Jerome H. Powell, the Fed chair, wants to keep the central bank’s policy options open as officials stare down another economic shock that threatens to stoke inflation and crimp growth.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair, is seeking flexibility for the central bank as it navigates the latest economic shock: the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.

item.title: What to Know About Section 702, the Controversial Warrantless Surveillance Law
item.description: A warrantless wiretapping law known as Section 702 is set to expire on April 20 unless Congress votes to extend it. Past cycles have been rocky.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage
item.category: Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of both parties are hoping to attach new limits on government surveillance and data collection to any legislation.

item.title: Attacks on Oil and Natural Gas Facilities Could Lead to Much Higher Prices
item.description: Attacks on oil and natural gas facilities this week could make it much harder for Persian Gulf countries to rebuild and restart production when the war eventually ends.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Altaf Qadri/Associated Press
item.media:description: Smoke rose from an oil site in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, on Saturday. Energy experts say more attacks could cause prices to surge.

item.title: Homeland Security Nominee Clears Key Hurdle
item.description: If confirmed, Senator Markwayne Mullin of Oklahoma would be taking over the department at a sensitive moment.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Markwayne Mullin, Republican of Oklahoma, has been a staunch defender of the Trump administration’s mass deportation campaign.

item.title: Elon Musk Whips Up Frenzy Against John Thune Over SAVE America Act
item.description: Elon Musk is furiously pushing for Senator John Thune, the majority leader, to pass a strict voter ID bill. But so far, he hasn’t publicly spent money to promote the legislation.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Markus Schreiber/Associated Press, Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Using his social media site X, Elon Musk has led a pressure campaign against Senator John Thune of South Dakota, the majority leader, over the SAVE America Act, a voting bill pushed by President Trump.

item.title: ‘Go Big and Go Loud’: Inside the Justice Dept.’s Push to Prosecute Protesters
item.description: Prosecutors have struggled to prove in court what the president and his aides have repeatedly said in public: that a network of leftist activists presents a serious threat to national security.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer, Alexandra Berzon and Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: David Guttenfelder/The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters marching through downtown Minneapolis to demonstrate against federal immigration operations in January.

item.title: Trump Says U.S. and Qatar Not Involved in Strike on Iran’s South Pars Gas Field
item.description: President Trump said Israel was responsible for the attack, but vowed to “massively” destroy the gas field if Tehran hit Qatar’s energy facilities in retaliation.
item.dc:creator: Aurelien Breeden
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said that Israel had “violently lashed out” at Iran’s South Pars gas field, hitting a “relatively small section” of the site.

item.title: Trump Vowed to Crack Down on Fraudsters, but He’s Pardoned Dozens
item.description: Across both of his terms, President Trump has granted clemency to more than 70 allies, donors and others convicted in fraud cases.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Purdy and Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
